title: Trade Embargo Compliance for Goods and Destination Checks

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'EU sanctions / trade controls' ‚Üí 'Art.9'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk UBA (2941), Splunk DB Connect (2686)
spl:
index=aml sourcetype="trade:finance:txn" earliest=-7d
| eval aml_tag="emb"
| eval uc_id="22.25.23"
| bin _time span=1h
| stats count as events, sum(eval(screening_result="hit")) as hits, median(screening_latency_ms) as lat by customer_id, aml_tag, _time
| eventstats perc95(amount_eur) as p95_amt by aml_tag
| where (aml_tag="struct" AND amount_eur>9000 AND amount_eur<10000) OR hits>0 OR lat>800
| sort - events
| head 300
description: Strengthens anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing monitoring, investigations, and supervisory examination readiness across transactions, customers, and list screening.
value: Strengthens anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing monitoring, investigations, and supervisory examination readiness across transactions, customers, and list screening.
implementation: (1) Normalize field extractions and CIM tags; (2) schedule hourly or near-real-time; (3) route alerts to SOC/compliance queues; (4) export evidence packs for supervisory review.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk UBA (2941), Splunk DB Connect (2686).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=aml` `trade:finance:txn` ‚Äî customer_id, amount_eur, screening_result, screening_latency_ms, list_version.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
(1) Normalize field extractions and CIM tags; (2) schedule hourly or near-real-time; (3) route alerts to SOC/compliance queues; (4) export evidence packs for supervisory review.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=aml sourcetype="trade:finance:txn" earliest=-7d
| eval aml_tag="emb"
| eval uc_id="22.25.23"
| bin _time span=1h
| stats count as events, sum(eval(screening_result="hit")) as hits, median(screening_latency_ms) as lat by customer_id, aml_tag, _time
| eventstats perc95(amount_eur) as p95_amt by aml_tag
| where (aml_tag="struct" AND amount_eur>9000 AND amount_eur<10000) OR hits>0 OR lat>800
| sort - events
| head 300
```

Understanding this SPL

**Trade Embargo Compliance for Goods and Destination Checks (EU sanctions / trade controls)** ‚Äî Strengthens anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing monitoring, investigations, and supervisory examination readiness across transactions, customers, and list screening.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=aml` `trade:finance:txn` ‚Äî customer_id, amount_eur, screening_result, screening_latency_ms, list_version. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk UBA (2941), Splunk DB Connect (2686). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: aml; **sourcetype**: trade:finance:txn. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=aml, sourcetype="trade:finance:txn", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **aml_tag** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **uc_id** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by customer_id, aml_tag, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by aml_tag** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where (aml_tag="struct" AND amount_eur>9000 AND amount_eur<10000) OR hits>0 OR lat>800` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
‚Ä¢ Limits the number of rows with `head`.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart (hits/lat), Histogram (amount_eur), Table (customer_id)
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
